who holds this power today and how they use it: “It must also be recognized that nuclear energy, biotechnology, information technology, knowledge of our own DNA, and many other abilities which we have acquired… have given those with the knowledge, and especially the economic resources to use them, an impressive dominance over the whole of humanity and the entire world.” [7] In the past, it was largely up to the State to guide and direct innovation. Today, however, the main drivers of development are private, often transnational, parties that are endowed with resources and the capacity to intervene that surpass those of many Governments. Technological power thus takes on an unprecedented, predominantly “private” aspect, which makes it even more challenging to discern, govern and direct such power toward the common good. I look forward to reading this in detail. As I get older (and perhaps as AI has allowed me to spend more time thinking and less time doing) I've found myself thinking more and more about what it means to live a virtuous life and about ethics and morality and so forth. I don't have any answers (and I'm not looking for them, really, just musing) but I do find it very interesting to read and learn from and about those whose job it is to think about the answer to those questions. Divided into five chapters, Magnifica humanitas has an underlying premise: technology is not “a force antagonistic to humanity” (4), nor is it “inherently evil” (9). However, “technology is never neutral, because it takes on the characteristics of those who devise, finance, regulate, and use it. Therefore, Pope Leo XIV appeals for people to build “for the common good” and to “remain human,” following a courageous mentality of shared responsibility and communion, so that the world “will come to recognize the human heart as the place where God desires to dwell” (16). AI must be “disarmed” in order to free it from the mentality of military, economic, and cognitive competition. “To disarm means discrediting the assumption that technical power automatically confers the right to govern,” he says. “To disarm does not mean rejecting technology, but preventing it from dominating humanity” (110). He devotes ample space to a critique of transhumanism and posthumanism, which interpret progress as the overcoming of human limits. Instead, limitations are not defects to be eliminated, but a constitutive dimension of the human person, because it is in fragility and finitude that relationship and openness to God and to others mature. He says we must remember that “humanity flourishes not despite limitations, but often through them” (118). Pursuing technological innovation at the expense of eliminating human limitations, he says, would cause an anthropological regression. “Humanity—in all its grandeur and woundedness—must never be replaced or surpassed,” he says. Technology can alleviate humanity’s sufferings and open new possibilities, but it must not deny the essence of humanity, which is our “capacity for relationship and love” (126). In the face of AI, says the Pope, “the true alternative is not between enthusiasm and fear, but between two paths of development: a progress that serves individuals and peoples, or a progress that subjects them to the mentality of power” (129). That's a long read. I grew up Catholic, went through a pretty devout few years in my early adulthood, but ultimately I have decided that it is not for me. I send my kids to a Catholic school though (it is deeply tied to our culture), so I guess in that sense it is still worth my time reading it in full. EDIT: Few paragraphs in, it is beautifully written. Interestingly, the Latin version of the encyclica is yet to be released at the moment >  a uniformity that eliminated diversity and that chose homogenization over communion  Unrelated to AI, but a wonderful support of the breadth of humanity in this anti-DEI time. >  We must, then, avoid the “Babel syndrome,” namely the idolatry of profit that sacrifices the weak, a uniformity that neutralizes differences, and the pretense that a single language — even a digital one — can translate everything, including the mystery of the person, into data and performance.  There is a lot to read here. I am curious where the meditations on the 'mystery of the person' will go: a brief search doesn't show further mention. The encyclical appears to focus on exhortations for us, humans, than on the nature of AI. Probably wise at this stage. I feel it is not AI that is either positive or negative, but its use of it, and the call-out to the growth of private industry as more powerful among nation-states is a strong statement for a institute like the Vatican to make: >  Technological power thus takes on an unprecedented, predominantly “private” aspect, which makes it even more challenging to discern, govern and direct such power toward the common good.  I have only skimmed it, will definitely read carefully as soon as I have time. I will say, as an atheist, that regarding technology the Vatican has some of the best takes of any institution/government I have ever seen. How many Catholics will put the encyclical to be summarized through an LLM?

demand_clusters.question_shape: How do I stop Claude Code agents from running expensive code without my approval?
demand_clusters.creator_brief: Show a live demo of a supervision layer built around Claude Code that intercepts agent actions before execution, using a simple approve/deny UI. Tie it to the $8k-$10k website build workflow so viewers see exactly where runaway agents eat into project profit margins.

demand_clusters.question_shape: Can AI agents actually remember my client preferences across multiple $10K website projects?
demand_clusters.creator_brief: Demonstrate a memory layer attached to a coding agent that stores client tone, stack preferences, and revision history, then auto-loads context on the next project. Frame this as a competitive moat for solo creators selling AI websites so buyers get a 'trained' agent that knows their brand.

demand_clusters.question_shape: What is the cheapest way to run AI inference for client websites I'm building to sell?
demand_clusters.creator_brief: Compare inference costs across DeepSeek, local-first models, and hosted APIs for the exact workloads that appear in a typical $8k-$10k AI website build. Show a monthly cost breakdown so solo creators know which stack keeps margins above 70% when delivering to clients.

demand_clusters.question_shape: Should I use Claude Code or Cursor to build and deliver AI websites to clients?
demand_clusters.creator_brief: Run a side-by-side build of the same client feature using Claude Code and Cursor, tracking time, token cost, and output quality, then declare a winner for the solo-creator-selling-AI-websites use case. Keep it under 60 seconds and end with a concrete recommendation tied to project size.

demand_clusters.question_shape: How do I build an MCP server that handles domain research for AI website clients automatically?
demand_clusters.creator_brief: Walk through spinning up a minimal MCP server that scrapes competitor domains, pulls SEO signals, and feeds structured output straight into a coding agent's context window. Position this as a billable discovery deliverable creators can package into their $8k-$10k website offer.

demand_clusters.question_shape: Can reusable agent skill blocks cut the time I spend building each AI website in half?
demand_clusters.creator_brief: Build a small library of three to five plug-and-play agent skills—auth flow, CMS scaffolding, payment integration—and show how composing them reduces build time on a demo client project. Frame the library as a personal IP asset that compounds in value the more websites the creator sells.

briefing.text:
**What moved**
**Agentic coding assistants** is the standout mover — velocity 4.29 with acceleration +1.60, pushing deep into hype stage (saturation 81). This is peaking *now*. Pair it with your Claude Code / coding agent content immediately. **AI agent memory systems** also accelerating (+0.80), still in creator stage with room to run — strong angle for Hermes Agent demos.

**What died**
Nothing hard-crashed today, but **data mixture auditing** (velocity 2.00, saturation 20) and **test-time finetuning** (velocity 3.00, saturation 24) remain low-engagement whisper-stage topics. Skip these — wrong audience for your $8k–$10k website angle.

**What's emerging**
**Agent cost and budget control** (velocity 4.29, saturation 39) and **MCP servers for domain tools** (saturation 49, builder stage) are both under-saturated with strong velocity. "How I control AI agent spend" hits your client-sales money angle perfectly. Strike before saturation climbs.

Local-first AI applications will reach the builder stage by Q4 2026 as privacy-conscious developers and enterprises begin shipping production systems that run inference on-device or private infrastructure.
Skill-driven agent engineering will enter the hype stage by Q4 2026 as Claude Code and similar agentic tools mature, attracting mainstream attention and competitive crowding.
AI code quality detection will reach the builder stage by Q4 2026 as Claude Code and similar agents mature, enabling creators to package detection workflows into sellable AI systems.
Claude Code vs Cursor comparisons will reach the builder stage by early July 2026 as more solo creators test both tools for client projects and begin sharing workflow tutorials.

Business process automation will transition from whisper to builder stage by Q3 2026 as AI agents like Claude Code and Hermes mature, enabling solo creators to build automation workflows commercially.
AI-native applications will transition from whisper to builder stage by Q3 2026 as more solo creators and small teams begin shipping production-ready AI agents and autonomous systems.
